food_subgroup: Noodle and congee dishes with seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee entry (FOODON_00002572). No specific mixed-seafood noodle/congee combination entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers rice porridge/congee as a category. No specific mixed-seafood noodle-congee hybrid code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for mixed seafood noodle/congee dish.

main_ingredients: mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish slices, clams, scallops)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of mixed seafood noodle/congee weighs approximately 450 g, comprising rice or noodle base, mixed seafood pieces, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood congee / rice porridge with seafood — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for seafood congee and seafood noodle soup
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches 'mixed seafood noodle/congee'. Values are adapted from HPB entries for seafood congee (approximately 65 kcal/100 g) and seafood noodle soup (approximately 68 kcal/100 g), weighted to reflect a 50/50 noodle-congee hybrid with mixed seafood. FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for seafood congee used as cross-reference. Macros reflect a broth-heavy, moderate-protein dish typical of Singaporean seafood congee/noodle preparations.

gi_source.title: Congee (rice porridge) glycemic index — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mixed seafood noodle/congee. GI of 38 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice congee/porridge (GI approximately 38, low category), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values. The addition of seafood protein and noodles is expected to maintain or slightly lower the GI relative to plain congee. GL per serving calculated as (38 × 38.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 14.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g bowl provides approximately 1710 mg sodium (71% of the 2400 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium seasoning, limit soy sauce additions, and consider sharing a portion or reducing serving frequency.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice or noodle base, partially offset by seafood protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 (low) and a GL of approximately 14.5 per bowl, this dish is moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is advised, and choosing a smaller serving or requesting extra seafood over extra noodles can further reduce postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1710 mg per bowl) and moderate cholesterol (~248 mg per bowl from mixed seafood) require attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease; opting for steamed or poached seafood variants with minimal added soy sauce and sesame oil can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Seafood Noodle / Congee (混合海鲜粉/粥) is a popular Singaporean Chinese comfort dish that combines two classic preparations: a silky, slow-cooked rice congee (porridge) base and/or soft rice vermicelli or wheat noodles, served in a savoury seafood broth and topped with an assortment of fresh mixed seafood such as prawns, squid rings, fish slices, clams, and scallops. The dish is typically seasoned with ginger, spring onion, white pepper, sesame oil, and light soy sauce, producing a mild, umami-rich flavour with a smooth, comforting texture. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at seafood congee stalls, zi char restaurants, and hawker centres, and is often ordered as a shared dish or as a light meal. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, high-moisture dish (approximately 66 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from the seafood (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g), low fat content (1.4 g per 100 g), and a moderate carbohydrate load from the rice or noodle base (8.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when portion sizes are controlled. Sodium content is elevated due to the broth and seasoning, which is a key consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides vitamin B12 and zinc from the seafood, supporting neurological and immune function.

Is seafood congee good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exactly matches 'mixed seafood noodle/congee' as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from closely related HPB Singapore Foodo entries for seafood congee and seafood noodle soup, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice congee. Confidence is medium rather than high because the dish is a hybrid preparation and exact composition varies by restaurant.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on HPB Singapore Foodo entries for seafood congee and seafood noodle soup; no single exact match exists for this hybrid dish. 2. The restaurant listed (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall) serves this as a hot-pot style seafood congee/noodle dish at SGD 33.16, suggesting a premium multi-seafood preparation; actual nutrient content may vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this dish category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this dish category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in this dish type per HPB source data. 7. Halal status is marked false as mixed seafood congee may include shellfish (clams) which are not universally accepted as halal; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice congee entry; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists.
